text: Here we present a case of transanal total mesorectal excision using near infrared light to assist in the join up between the abdominal and transanal components. The patient was a thirty seven year old male referred from a secondary centre with a low rectal cancer about one to one point five centimetres above the anal rectal junction. MRI staging staged the tumour at T-to-N-zero, and the patient didn't have any neoadjuvant treatment.

The abdominal team uses a standard endo-eye laparoscope to mobilize the colon and begin total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection. Concurrently, the transanal team employs a fluorescence-enabled pinpoint laparoscope to dissect cranially past the pelvic floor.

The surgeon activates the near-infrared mode, revealing a signal in the anterior plane of dissection. The standard laparoscope emits near-infrared light, which penetrates tissue more effectively than white light, making the signal visible in the dissection area.
The surgical field is illuminated by near-infrared light as the abdominal team removes their light source from the abdomen, maintaining visibility of the dissection area.

The abdominal light source is reintroduced, enhancing the near-infrared light signal visible on the pinpoint display. As the abdominal and transanal teams approach the junction, the near-infrared signal intensifies, indicating proximity.
